food_subgroup: Crustaceans — freshwater prawn, grilled or steamed with sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for giant freshwater scampi prawn with sauce. FOODON_03311737 (shrimp food product) is the closest parent class covering crustacean seafood products.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers crustaceans broadly. No specific code exists for giant freshwater prawn prepared with oozing sauce in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144001 covers prawn as a substance. No specific code for giant scampi prawn with sauce preparation exists.

serving.description: 2 whole giant scampi prawns with sauce
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving consists of 2 whole giant freshwater scampi prawns (approximately 400 g total with shell), served with an oozing garlic-butter or chilli-oyster sauce typical of Thai seafood restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Macrobrachium rosenbergii (Giant Freshwater Prawn), cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 15150 / analogous cooked shrimp with sauce adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for giant scampi prawn with oozing sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central under this exact preparation. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked shrimp / freshwater prawn data (NDB 15150, cooked moist heat) with upward adjustment to sodium and fat to account for the sauce component (garlic-butter or chilli-oyster sauce). Energy, protein, and carbohydrate are anchored to the USDA cooked prawn baseline. Sauce contribution estimated from USDA condiment entries. FAO/INFOODS SEA tables for Macrobrachium rosenbergii were also consulted as a cross-reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — shellfish and seafood reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: Giant freshwater scampi prawn is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate food. No direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. GI is estimated at 5 based on the University of Sydney GI Database convention for high-protein, negligible-carbohydrate seafood foods (analogous to cooked shrimp and other crustaceans). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (5 × 7.2) / 100 ≈ 0.4, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 7.2 g.

hypertension.guidance: The oozing sauce (garlic-butter or chilli-oyster sauce) contributes approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one prawn and request reduced-sauce preparation to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2,000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only approximately 1.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a glycemic index estimated at 5, this dish has a negligible glycemic load and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content and sodium from sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Giant freshwater prawn contains approximately 152 mg cholesterol per 100 g, which is moderate; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion to one prawn, choose steamed over butter-based preparation, and monitor total saturated fat and sodium intake from the sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Giant Scampi Prawn with Oozing Sauce (2 pieces) is a Thai seafood dish featuring two whole giant freshwater prawns (Macrobrachium rosenbergii), each typically weighing 150–250 g, served with a rich oozing sauce made from garlic, butter or oil, chilli, oyster sauce, and fish sauce. The prawns are commonly grilled or steamed until the shell turns bright orange-red and the flesh is firm yet succulent. The oozing sauce seeps into the prawn cavity, creating an intensely savoury, slightly spicy, and umami-rich flavour profile. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai seafood restaurants and is considered a premium item due to the large size and quality of the prawns. Nutritionally, giant freshwater prawn is an excellent source of lean protein (approximately 20 g per 100 g), selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and omega-3 fatty acids. The dish is low in carbohydrates and has a negligible glycemic index, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. However, the sauce contributes significant sodium (approximately 420 mg per 100 g), which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Cholesterol content is moderate at approximately 152 mg per 100 g. The dish is naturally gluten-free in its prawn component but the oyster sauce may introduce wheat-derived gluten. It is halal-suitable when prepared without alcohol-based ingredients.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for giant scampi prawn with oozing sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked freshwater prawn data with sauce-component adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database conventions for high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood. Confidence is medium rather than low because the prawn baseline is well-supported by USDA data and the sauce adjustment is conservative and documented.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp/prawn entry and no reliable value is available for this preparation. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) for the prawn flesh; trace amounts may be present from butter in the sauce but are below detection threshold and reported as 0.0 (trace). Dietary fibre is 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting minimal fibre from sauce ingredients; this is a confirmed near-zero value consistent with USDA data for cooked crustaceans. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate for 2 whole giant prawns including shell; edible flesh yield is approximately 50–60%, giving approximately 200–240 g edible portion. Sodium value of 420 mg per 100 g reflects the sauce contribution and is an adapted estimate; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. GI set to 5 as an analogous estimate for a high-protein, negligible-carbohydrate seafood dish per University of Sydney GI Database conventions; gi_category is low. The food_id GD001293 is preserved from the field survey data seed record. Price SGD 93.80 is preserved from the source record for reference only and is not used in nutritional scoring.
